turns.content: I'm following a tutorial on immutable data and I thought everything that needs to be shared across threads should just use the const keyword instead of static, so I tried writing my config struct with const items but it won't compile.

That's a common misunderstanding. While const is inlined at every use site and great for compile-time constants like the length of an array or function parameters, static has one address shared across all references to it. Think of const items as read-only variables that exist only on the type definition, whereas static items are actual values stored in memory that can be accessed by multiple parts of your program at runtime.
Ah okay so if I want to share a single configuration object across my application modules, should I use const then?

Not quite. You cannot put actual data values in const items; you can only put compile-time constants there. For shared runtime data like your config object, you need static or a global variable. If you want threadsafe immutable access to that shared data, you'd use &'static T from static items instead of trying to make it const.
Wait so I can't just add mut to my config and call it static?

You cannot use mut static items without unsafe; the language guarantees that static items have a single address, but if you try to mutate them from multiple threads or locations, you risk data races. The only safe way to modify a static item is through a single-threaded path or using unsafe blocks with proper memory ordering.
What about lifetimes then? Can I use &'static T on my config struct?

Yes, you can have &'static T references to static items because those items live for the entire duration of the program. This is how you achieve thread safety and long-lived immutability in Rust without needing to box or use smart pointers unnecessarily.
So my tutorial was misleading me into thinking const would work like static for actual data sharing?

Exactly. The tutorial probably simplified things or confused the two concepts. Remember that const is about compile-time constants while static is about runtime shared memory. For your config struct, define it as a static variable and then use &'static Config references throughout your code.
Got it, so I'll change my approach to using a static global with &'static references instead of trying const for the actual data.

That's the right path. Just make sure your initialization happens once at startup and you never attempt to mutate that static item without unsafe. Your config will now be properly thread-safe and long-lived with the correct lifetime annotations.

I'm trying to build a configuration loader for my automation tool, but I keep getting confused about when to use `static` versus `const`. Here's the code I wrote: let config = Config::new(); where Config uses a static field. It feels like it should be const since the value never changes at runtime.

You're hitting that classic beginner trap where `const` and `static` feel interchangeable because they both hold immutable data. The key difference is that `const` items are inlined at every use site, so if you have a huge config table as a const, it bloats the binary everywhere it's referenced. Static items, on the other hand, live in memory with one address and can be much larger without impacting code size.
That makes sense for size, but I'm still stuck on this part: static field = SomeValue. Why does my compiler complain about it? Is there something wrong with how I declared the variable?

Ah, if you're trying to assign a mutable value to a `static` item in stable Rust without using `unsafe`, that's where things get tricky. You can't just write static mut field = Value; because the compiler won't let you mutate it at runtime unless you drop `unsafe`. The `&'static T` from static items is safe only if the data never changes after initialization.
So I can't just use `static mut` like in C? But what if my config needs to be updated later during runtime?

Exactly, you'd need to move that into a heap-allocated struct or use an Arc or Rc wrapper if you want thread safety. If your tool is single-threaded and the config never changes after startup, just keep it as a regular `static` without mut. But remember: `const` is only for compile-time constants like version numbers or array lengths.
Got it, so my automation tool should probably use static for the config since it's loaded once at startup and never changes. Can I still reference it from multiple modules without copying?

Yes, because `static` items have a single address in memory, so any module can reference them without duplication or inlining overhead. Just make sure they're initialized before main starts and avoid trying to mutate them later unless you switch to unsafe blocks.
Thanks! One last question: what's the best practice for organizing these in my project structure?

Put your `static` items near the top of your main crate module, maybe in a config.rs file with clear comments about initialization order. Keep `const` values scattered where they're actually used since they don't add up time overhead to the binary.
